'''Gene Robert Ngamu'''<ref>[http://nzleague.co.nz/kiwis/player_profile.php?letter=N&id=642 NGAMU, GENE ROBERT 1993 - 99 - KIWI #642] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120402171241/http://nzleague.co.nz/kiwis/player_profile.php?letter=N&id=642 |date=2012-04-02 }} ''nzleague.co.nz''</ref> (born 27 January 1974) is a New Zealand former [[rugby league]] footballer. He played mainly as a {{rlp|hb}} or {{rlp|fe}}, and is probably best known for his combination with [[Stacey Jones]] at the [[Auckland Warriors]] and the [[New Zealand national rugby league team|Kiwis]].

==Early years==
Ngamu started his career at the [[Marist Saints]] and [[Northcote Tigers]] in the [[Auckland Rugby League]] competition.<ref>Richard Becht. ''A New Breed Rising: The Warriors Winfield Cup Challenge''. Auckland, HarperCollins, 1994. {{ISBN|1-86950-154-3}}. p.183</ref> He joined the Tigers in the 1992 pre-season while on a scholarship with the [[Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles]], however was called to Sydney full-time before the season began.<ref name=akl>Lion Red Rugby League Annual 1992 ''New Zealand Rugby League'', 1992. p.p.131-143</ref>

==Australian competition==
In 1992, he moved to [[Sydney]], signing with the [[Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles]] on a two-year deal. He made the [[Junior Kiwis]] in 1992.{{fact|date=April 2022}}

In 1994, he joined [[South Sydney Rabbitohs|South Sydney]] but could not break into the first-grade side.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.ssralmanac.com/mobile/players_n/player_ngamu_gene.htm|title=South Sydney Rabbitohs Rugby League Player Report -Gene Ngamu|website=www.ssralmanac.com}}</ref>

He returned home in 1995 to join the new [[Auckland Warriors]] franchise, playing in the inaugural run on side. A goalkicker, Ngamu holds the record for Most Points in a Match for the Auckland Warriors, a feat only equaled by [[Ivan Cleary]] and [[James Maloney (rugby league)|James Maloney]]. He stayed with the Warriors until the 1999 season, when he left for England. Ngamu was selected for the [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand team]] to compete in the end of season [[1999 Rugby League Tri-Nations]] tournament. In the final against [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]] he played from the interchange bench in the Kiwis' 22-20 loss.{{fact|date=April 2022}}

==England==
Ngamu had an unhappy two seasons with the [[Huddersfield Giants|Huddersfield-Sheffield]] joint franchise before leaving England.<ref name=coffey>Coffey and Wood ''The Kiwis: 100 Years of International Rugby League'' {{ISBN|1-86971-090-8}}</ref>

==Representative career==
Ngamu made his international début for the [[New Zealand national rugby league team]] in 1993, one year after his [[Junior Kiwis]] debut.

He played in two [[Rugby League World Cup|World Cups]], representing the Kiwis in 1995 and the [[New Zealand Māori rugby league team|Aotearoa Māori]] in 2000.

==Later years==
Ngamu then joined a [[rugby union]] club in [[Manly, New South Wales|Manly]] before retiring from professional football. In 2005 he played for the [[North Harbour Dolphins]] in the [[Auckland Rugby League|Phelan Shield]] before being called into the [[Northcote Tigers#North Harbour Tigers|North Harbour Tigers]] team mid way through the [[2005 Bartercard Cup]].<ref>[http://www.highbeam.com/doc/1P2-12170784.html Ngamu gets Tigers call-up ]{{dead link|date=February 2019|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}} ''The Press'', 5 August 2005</ref> Gene then retired with his wife Fiona and had a family.
